prompt: In John 5, what was the Hebrew name of the pool by the sheep gate?
choices_json: Bethesda
Siloam
Gabbatha
Bethany
correct_answer: Bethesda
explanation: The pool by the sheep gate was called Bethesda in Hebrew (John 5:2).

prompt: How long had the sick man at the pool been ill in John 5?
choices_json: Thirty-eight years
Twelve years
Forty years without relief
Seven years
correct_answer: Thirty-eight years
explanation: John says the man had been sick for thirty-eight years (John 5:5).

prompt: What did Jesus tell the sick man to take up in John 5?
choices_json: His mat
His cloak
A water jar
His staff
correct_answer: His mat
explanation: Jesus told him, "Arise, take up your mat, and walk" (John 5:8).

prompt: On what day did the healing in John 5 take place?
choices_json: The Sabbath
The Passover
The Day of Preparation
The Feast of Booths
correct_answer: The Sabbath
explanation: John notes that the healing happened on the Sabbath (John 5:9).

prompt: Where did Jesus find the healed man afterward in John 5?
choices_json: In the temple
At the pool
In Capernaum
By the sheep gate
correct_answer: In the temple
explanation: Jesus later found the healed man in the temple (John 5:14).

prompt: Whom did the healed man tell the Jews had made him well?
choices_json: Jesus
John the Baptist
An angel
Moses
correct_answer: Jesus
explanation: The man went away and told the Jews that Jesus had made him well (John 5:15).

prompt: What object did the Jews say it was unlawful to carry on that day?
choices_json: A mat
A basket
A lamp
A loaf
correct_answer: A mat
explanation: They objected that it was not lawful to carry the mat on the Sabbath (John 5:10).

prompt: How many porches did the pool called Bethesda have in John 5?
choices_json: Five
Four
Six
Twelve
correct_answer: Five
explanation: Bethesda is described as having five porches (John 5:2).

prompt: Why did the sick man say he had not gotten into the pool in time?
choices_json: He had no one to put him in when the water was stirred
He was waiting for the priests to arrive
He was too weak to beat the others into the water
He was afraid to step in first
correct_answer: He had no one to put him in when the water was stirred
explanation: He said no one would put him into the pool when the water was stirred up (John 5:7).

prompt: What exact question did Jesus ask the man before healing him in John 5?
choices_json: Do you want to be made well?
Do you believe in the Son of God?
Why are you angry with me?
Do you seek the glory of God?
correct_answer: Do you want to be made well?
explanation: Jesus asked him, "Do you want to be made well?" (John 5:6).

prompt: What warning did Jesus give the healed man in the temple?
choices_json: Sin no more, so that nothing worse happens to you.
Do not carry your mat again before the temple rulers.
Tell no one who healed you in this place.
Return tomorrow and wash again in the pool.
correct_answer: Sin no more, so that nothing worse happens to you.
explanation: Jesus warned him, "Sin no more, so that nothing worse happens to you" (John 5:14).

prompt: According to John 5:18, what claim made the Jews seek all the more to kill Jesus?
choices_json: He called God his own Father
He denied the authority of Moses
He said the temple had failed
He refused to answer the priests
correct_answer: He called God his own Father
explanation: John says they reacted because Jesus called God his own Father, making himself equal with God (John 5:18).

prompt: To whom did Jesus say the Father had given all judgment in John 5?
choices_json: The Son
The Father who sent him
John
Israel
correct_answer: The Son
explanation: Jesus said the Father has given all judgment to the Son (John 5:22).

prompt: Which witness did Jesus say the Jewish leaders had sent to in John 5?
choices_json: John
Elijah
Nicodemus
The healed man
correct_answer: John
explanation: Jesus said, "You have sent to John, and he has testified to the truth" (John 5:33).

prompt: Why did carrying the mat become such a public flashpoint in John 5?
choices_json: It made the Sabbath healing impossible for the authorities to ignore
It proved the pool had stirred at the right moment
It showed the man had paid his temple offering
It invited the crowd to follow Jesus into the temple courts
correct_answer: It made the Sabbath healing impossible for the authorities to ignore
explanation: The carried mat made the Sabbath dispute visible and pushed the healing into public controversy (John 5:9-10).

prompt: What does Jesus' reply, "My Father is still working, so I am working, too," imply in John 5?
choices_json: His work shares in the Father's ongoing work
He plans to stop healing after the feast
He is only copying what Moses once did in the wilderness
His task is limited to the pool at Bethesda
correct_answer: His work shares in the Father's ongoing work
explanation: Jesus links his action with the Father's continuing work, which is why the claim sounds so serious in context (John 5:17-18).

prompt: Why is it significant that the healed man did not know Jesus' name at first in John 5?
choices_json: The sign reached him before he understood who Jesus was
The crowd had confused Jesus with one of the officers
The man had refused to answer Jesus directly
The temple leaders had already hidden Jesus' identity from everyone there
correct_answer: The sign reached him before he understood who Jesus was
explanation: John notes that the man was healed before he recognized Jesus personally (John 5:13).

prompt: What contrast does Jesus draw when he says the Scriptures testify about him, yet people refuse to come to him?
choices_json: Bible searching can miss its goal if it does not lead to Jesus
The writings of Moses are less important than the pool
Only the healed man understood the Scriptures correctly
John's testimony matters more than every written witness in Israel
correct_answer: Bible searching can miss its goal if it does not lead to Jesus
explanation: Jesus says they search the Scriptures but still refuse to come to him for life (John 5:39-40).

prompt: Why does Jesus appeal to John, his works, the Father, and the Scriptures as witnesses in John 5?
choices_json: He answers the charge that his testimony stands alone
He wants to rank the best source of evidence for the crowd
He is preparing the healed man to speak before the rulers
He is comparing four different feasts in Jerusalem
correct_answer: He answers the charge that his testimony stands alone
explanation: After mentioning that self-testimony is disputed, Jesus points to several witnesses that confirm him (John 5:31-39).

prompt: In John 5:24, what change happens to the one who hears Jesus' word and believes?
choices_json: That person has passed from death into life
That person becomes a judge over Israel
That person is healed after entering the pool
That person is freed from every earthly illness
correct_answer: That person has passed from death into life
explanation: Jesus says the believer has passed out of death into life (John 5:24).

prompt: At the end of John 5, how does Moses function in Jesus' argument?
choices_json: Moses becomes an accuser because his writings point to Jesus
Moses replaces John as the witness whose voice settles the case
Moses is presented as the one who healed on the Sabbath
Moses proves that signs matter more than obedience
correct_answer: Moses becomes an accuser because his writings point to Jesus
explanation: Jesus says Moses will accuse them because Moses wrote about him (John 5:45-47).

prompt: How does the healing at Bethesda function within the larger argument of John 5?
choices_json: It opens into a discourse about the Son's authority to give life and judge
It mainly explains why the crowds preferred Jerusalem during feast season
It shows that the pool remained the center of healing after Jesus left
It serves as a quiet sign with little connection to what follows
correct_answer: It opens into a discourse about the Son's authority to give life and judge
explanation: The healing leads directly into Jesus' claims about life, judgment, and the Father's work (John 5:8-29).

prompt: Why does John place the Sabbath dispute right beside Jesus' claims about the Father and the Son in John 5?
choices_json: The controversy over rest becomes the setting for revealing Jesus' divine authority
The argument is meant to prove the pool had lost its power forever
The chapter shifts away from healing and focuses only on debates about Moses
The dispute shows that the temple leaders accepted the miracle at once
correct_answer: The controversy over rest becomes the setting for revealing Jesus' divine authority
explanation: The Sabbath conflict becomes the doorway into Jesus' claims about working with the Father (John 5:16-23).

prompt: What does John 5:23 mean when it says all should honor the Son just as they honor the Father?
choices_json: The Son is owed the same honor because he shares the Father's mission
The Son receives honor only after the Father steps back from judgment
The Son represents the temple but not the Father himself
The Son honors the Father by refusing all public recognition
correct_answer: The Son is owed the same honor because he shares the Father's mission
explanation: Jesus says failure to honor the Son is failure to honor the Father who sent him (John 5:23).

prompt: Why is the repeated language of witness important in John 5?
choices_json: It shows unbelief persists even when multiple testimonies agree
It means only John the Baptist can identify the Messiah correctly
It proves the healed man became Jesus' main spokesman in Jerusalem
It turns the chapter into a debate about Roman legal procedure
correct_answer: It shows unbelief persists even when multiple testimonies agree
explanation: John, Jesus' works, the Father, and the Scriptures all testify, yet the leaders still refuse him (John 5:32-40).

prompt: How do John 5:25 and John 5:28-29 work together in this chapter?
choices_json: Jesus' voice gives life now and will summon the dead from tombs later
They limit resurrection language to the healing at Bethesda alone
They separate the Father's voice from the Son's voice in public ministry
They show that judgment belongs to Moses rather than to the Son
correct_answer: Jesus' voice gives life now and will summon the dead from tombs later
explanation: The chapter joins present life for hearers with future resurrection for those in the tombs (John 5:25, 28-29).

prompt: What does Jesus finding the man in the temple add to the meaning of the healing in John 5?
choices_json: The restored man is back in worship, and the encounter adds a moral warning
The temple visit proves the man had always known Jesus' identity
The temple scene shows that the crowd forced Jesus to return to the pool
The meeting explains that carrying the mat was actually required by the law
correct_answer: The restored man is back in worship, and the encounter adds a moral warning
explanation: Jesus meets him in the temple and connects restored wholeness with the call to stop sinning (John 5:14).

prompt: How does John 5 portray the roots of unbelief among Jesus' opponents?
choices_json: They seek human glory and refuse the witness already given to them
They fail because the miracle at Bethesda was too private to verify
They are confused mainly because Jesus never mentions the Father
They would have believed if John had come back to testify again
correct_answer: They seek human glory and refuse the witness already given to them
explanation: Jesus says they receive glory from one another and do not have God's love in them (John 5:42-44).

prompt: What contrast does John 5 set up between the pool's hoped-for healing and Jesus' word?
choices_json: Jesus heals immediately by command, without dependence on stirred water
The pool grants life while Jesus offers only temporary relief
Jesus uses the pool as the final source of authority for healing in Jerusalem
The man is healed only after the water and Jesus work together
correct_answer: Jesus heals immediately by command, without dependence on stirred water
explanation: The man does not reach the water at all; Jesus' word alone makes him well (John 5:7-9).

prompt: How does John 5 move from one healed body to a claim about the whole human race?
choices_json: The sign leads to Jesus speaking about resurrection, judgment, and life for all
The narrative leaves the healed man behind and turns only to debates about temple customs
The miracle is repeated many times until the rulers accept it
The pool becomes a picture of how every feast in Jerusalem works
correct_answer: The sign leads to Jesus speaking about resurrection, judgment, and life for all
explanation: After healing one man, Jesus speaks about all the dead hearing his voice and rising (John 5:21-29).

prompt: What does John 5:26 suggest when it says the Father granted the Son to have life in himself?
choices_json: The Son shares the Father's own life-giving fullness
The Son received life only after the man at Bethesda was healed
The Son depends on the pool to display divine power
The Son will one day replace the Father's authority entirely
correct_answer: The Son shares the Father's own life-giving fullness
explanation: Jesus describes the Son as possessing life in himself, echoing the Father's life-giving identity (John 5:26).

prompt: How does John 5 define faithful reading of Scripture?
choices_json: Scripture should lead people to Jesus, the one it testifies about
Scripture matters only when a public sign explains every verse
Scripture and Moses belong to an older stage that Jesus ends
Scripture is useful chiefly for identifying feast days in Jerusalem
correct_answer: Scripture should lead people to Jesus, the one it testifies about
explanation: Jesus says the Scriptures testify about him, yet his hearers will not come to him for life (John 5:39-40).

prompt: Why is it ironic that Moses is said to accuse Jesus' opponents in John 5?
choices_json: The figure they rely on is the very one whose writings point to Jesus
Moses had publicly defended the healed man before the rulers
The leaders thought Moses had written against healing on the Sabbath
Moses is named because he also waited beside Bethesda
correct_answer: The figure they rely on is the very one whose writings point to Jesus
explanation: Jesus says that if they believed Moses, they would believe him, because Moses wrote about him (John 5:45-46).

prompt: According to John 5:44, what blocks these leaders from believing?
choices_json: They prize glory from one another more than glory from the only God
They lack access to the pool, the temple, and the strongest witnesses
They cannot remember what John the Baptist told them
They do not understand why the feast was held in Jerusalem
correct_answer: They prize glory from one another more than glory from the only God
explanation: Jesus ties their unbelief to seeking glory from one another instead of from the only God (John 5:44).

prompt: How does John 5 deepen John's portrait of Jesus after the sign at Bethesda?
choices_json: He is shown as the Son who works with the Father, gives life, and judges
He is presented mainly as a teacher whose words matter less than his signs
He is reduced to a healer whose mission stays local to one feast day
He is compared with John only to show that both have equal authority
correct_answer: He is shown as the Son who works with the Father, gives life, and judges
explanation: The chapter expands the healing sign into a portrait of Jesus as the life-giving and judging Son (John 5:17-29).

prompt: In John 6, which disciple did Jesus ask about buying bread for the crowd?
choices_json: Philip
Andrew
Peter
Judas
correct_answer: Philip
explanation: Jesus asked Philip where they should buy bread for the crowd (John 6:5).

prompt: What food did the boy have in John 6?
choices_json: Five barley loaves and two fish
Seven wheat loaves and three fish
Two barley loaves and five fish
Twelve loaves and one fish
correct_answer: Five barley loaves and two fish
explanation: Andrew mentions a boy with five barley loaves and two fish (John 6:9).

prompt: About how many men sat down for the meal in John 6?
choices_json: About five thousand
About three thousand
About seven thousand
About twelve thousand
correct_answer: About five thousand
explanation: John says the men sat down, in number about five thousand (John 6:10).

prompt: How many baskets of pieces were left over after the feeding in John 6?
choices_json: Twelve
Seven full baskets after everyone ate
Five
Two
correct_answer: Twelve
explanation: The disciples filled twelve baskets with broken pieces (John 6:13).

prompt: Where were the disciples heading by boat after evening came in John 6?
choices_json: Capernaum
Jerusalem
Bethsaida
Tiberias
correct_answer: Capernaum
explanation: The disciples entered the boat and were going across the sea to Capernaum (John 6:17).

prompt: What did Jesus say as he came near the boat on the sea in John 6?
choices_json: It is I. Don't be afraid.
Take courage and cast your nets.
Peace be to this house.
Follow me to the mountain.
correct_answer: It is I. Don't be afraid.
explanation: When the disciples were afraid, Jesus said, "It is I. Don't be afraid" (John 6:20).

prompt: What title did Jesus use for himself in John 6:35?
choices_json: The bread of life
The light of the world
The good shepherd
The resurrection and the life
correct_answer: The bread of life
explanation: Jesus says, "I am the bread of life" in John 6:35.

prompt: Which feast was near when Jesus fed the multitude in John 6?
choices_json: The Passover
The Feast of Booths
Pentecost
The Dedication
correct_answer: The Passover
explanation: John notes that the Passover, the feast of the Jews, was at hand (John 6:4).

prompt: Why did Jesus ask Philip where to buy bread in John 6?
choices_json: He was testing him
He needed money from him
He wanted Andrew's opinion first
He was sending him into the town
correct_answer: He was testing him
explanation: John says Jesus asked Philip this to test him, since he knew what he would do (John 6:6).

prompt: How much bread did Philip say would not be enough for the crowd in John 6?
choices_json: Two hundred denarii worth
Fifty shekels worth
Thirty pieces of silver
Twelve talents for a crowd this large
correct_answer: Two hundred denarii worth
explanation: Philip answered that two hundred denarii worth of bread would not be sufficient (John 6:7).

prompt: After rowing on the sea, how far had the disciples gone when they saw Jesus in John 6?
choices_json: About twenty-five or thirty stadia
About ten or twelve stadia
About forty or fifty stadia in the dark
About seventy stadia
correct_answer: About twenty-five or thirty stadia
explanation: John says they had rowed about twenty-five or thirty stadia when they saw Jesus (John 6:19).

prompt: What were the people planning to do after they saw the feeding sign in John 6?
choices_json: Make Jesus king by force
Ask Jesus to stay in Tiberias
Bring the sick to the mountain
Return at once to Jerusalem
correct_answer: Make Jesus king by force
explanation: Jesus knew they were about to come and take him by force to make him king (John 6:15).

prompt: According to John 6:29, what is the work of God?
choices_json: To believe in him whom he has sent
To gather the leftover baskets carefully
To keep the feast in Jerusalem
To search the Scriptures for manna
correct_answer: To believe in him whom he has sent
explanation: Jesus answers, "This is the work of God, that you believe in him whom he has sent" (John 6:29).

prompt: Where did Jesus say these things about the bread of life in John 6?
choices_json: In the synagogue in Capernaum
On the mountain above Galilee
In the temple courts in Jerusalem
At the shore near Tiberias
correct_answer: In the synagogue in Capernaum
explanation: John says Jesus taught these things in the synagogue in Capernaum (John 6:59).

prompt: What does Jesus say truly motivated the crowd's search for him the next day in John 6?
choices_json: They wanted more bread, not the sign's meaning
They feared the storm on the sea
They hoped to hear Andrew explain the sign again
They planned to leave Galilee for Judea
correct_answer: They wanted more bread, not the sign's meaning
explanation: Jesus says they sought him because they ate the loaves and were filled (John 6:26).

prompt: Why does Jesus tell the disciples to gather the broken pieces so that nothing be lost in John 6?
choices_json: The sign shows abundant provision without waste
The crowd still needed food for the trip to Jerusalem
The fragments had to be returned to the boy
The baskets were required for the synagogue service
correct_answer: The sign shows abundant provision without waste
explanation: The leftovers underline both abundance and care after everyone has eaten their fill (John 6:12-13).

prompt: What does the crowd's wish to make Jesus king show about their reading of the sign in John 6?
choices_json: They want a provider-king more than they grasp his mission
They understand at once that he must suffer and ascend
They see the sign mainly as a call to return to Jerusalem
They recognize that the miracle was done only for the twelve
correct_answer: They want a provider-king more than they grasp his mission
explanation: Their reaction focuses on political and material hopes rather than on who Jesus is (John 6:14-15).

prompt: How does Jesus contrast the manna in the wilderness with the bread he gives in John 6?
choices_json: Manna sustained for a time, but Jesus gives life that endures
Manna came from the Father, while Jesus came by Moses' provision
Manna filled more baskets than Jesus could provide
Manna was spiritual, but Jesus offers only earthly food
correct_answer: Manna sustained for a time, but Jesus gives life that endures
explanation: Jesus says the fathers ate manna and died, but the one who comes to him lives (John 6:49-51, 58).

prompt: Why do many disciples call Jesus' teaching a hard saying in John 6?
choices_json: His words about eating his flesh and drinking his blood offend them
He refuses to heal anyone else beside the sea
He tells them they must row back across the lake at night
His teaching can only be understood by trained teachers of the law in Jerusalem
correct_answer: His words about eating his flesh and drinking his blood offend them
explanation: Many disciples stumble over Jesus' language about eating his flesh and drinking his blood (John 6:52-60).

prompt: What does Peter's answer to Jesus, "To whom would we go?" reveal in John 6?
choices_json: He sees Jesus as the only source of words of eternal life
He expects Jesus to accept the crowd's offer of kingship in Galilee
He believes Andrew's food plan had been the right one
He wants the twelve to return to fishing in Galilee
correct_answer: He sees Jesus as the only source of words of eternal life
explanation: Peter stays because he believes Jesus has the words of eternal life (John 6:68).

prompt: How does John 6:65 explain why some people do not come to Jesus?
choices_json: Coming to him must be given by the Father
Only those with enough bread can hear him rightly
The crowd is waiting for another public sign first
They have not yet crossed the sea to Capernaum
correct_answer: Coming to him must be given by the Father
explanation: Jesus says no one can come to him unless it is given by the Father (John 6:65).

prompt: How does the feeding sign prepare for the Bread of Life discourse in John 6?
choices_json: It moves the crowd from physical bread to the question of lasting life
It shifts attention away from Jesus and onto the disciples' planning for the crowd
It proves that Galilee has replaced Jerusalem as the holy city
It shows that the boy's gift was greater than Jesus' teaching
correct_answer: It moves the crowd from physical bread to the question of lasting life
explanation: The miracle creates the setting for Jesus to redirect hunger toward the food that remains to eternal life (John 6:11-14, 27, 35).

prompt: Why does John mention that Passover was near at the start of John 6?
choices_json: It brings Exodus and manna themes into the background of the sign
It explains why the disciples had brought extra baskets that day
It shows that the crowd was already on the way to the temple courts
It marks the final week before Jesus' arrest in this Gospel
correct_answer: It brings Exodus and manna themes into the background of the sign
explanation: Passover makes the chapter's bread, wilderness, and Moses language especially resonant (John 6:4, 31-32).

prompt: What does Jesus claim by saying, "I am the bread of life," in John 6?
choices_json: He himself is the Father's life-giving provision from heaven
He is repeating Moses' role without changing anything
He only means that his teaching is harder than manna
He is speaking about the synagogue bread in Capernaum
correct_answer: He himself is the Father's life-giving provision from heaven
explanation: Jesus identifies himself, not merely his gifts, as the true bread from heaven (John 6:32-35).

prompt: How does John 6 connect faith with the Father's action?
choices_json: Coming and believing are described as enabled by the Father
Faith is replaced by crowd enthusiasm after the miracle meal
The Father acts only after people fully understand the sign
Belief is limited to those who helped gather the leftovers
correct_answer: Coming and believing are described as enabled by the Father
explanation: Jesus ties coming, believing, drawing, and giving to the Father's work (John 6:37, 44, 65).

prompt: What role does offense play in the structure of John 6?
choices_json: The hard teaching exposes the difference between curiosity and true discipleship
It shows that the crowd finally understands the miracle correctly
It proves the sea crossing was more difficult than the feeding itself
It ends the chapter by resolving every question about Jesus' identity
correct_answer: The hard teaching exposes the difference between curiosity and true discipleship
explanation: Many disciples leave over Jesus' words, while the twelve are pressed to respond in faith (John 6:60-69).

prompt: Why does John record both the crowd's enthusiasm and their later complaint in John 6?
choices_json: The chapter contrasts sign-seeking excitement with resistance to Jesus' claims
It explains why the disciples forgot the feeding by evening
It shows that the synagogue in Capernaum controlled the crowd
It proves that miracles alone guarantee lasting faith
correct_answer: The chapter contrasts sign-seeking excitement with resistance to Jesus' claims
explanation: The same crowd that praises the sign struggles when Jesus explains its meaning (John 6:14-15, 30, 41-42, 52).

prompt: How do the sea episode and the bread discourse work together in John 6?
choices_json: Both scenes reveal Jesus as more than a miracle worker, drawing fear and faith
The sea episode interrupts the chapter and has little link to the discourse
Both scenes are mainly about Philip learning to manage crowds
The two scenes are included only to explain the move to Capernaum
correct_answer: Both scenes reveal Jesus as more than a miracle worker, drawing fear and faith
explanation: John pairs the feeding and the sea crossing to show Jesus' authority before the discourse presses people to believe (John 6:19-21, 35-40).

prompt: How does John 6 reshape messianic expectation after the crowd tries to make Jesus king?
choices_json: Jesus refuses political seizure and offers himself as the true gift from heaven
Jesus accepts the title king but delays the public ceremony until Passover
Jesus hands the kingdom over to the twelve beside the sea
Jesus moves from signs to silence so no one can know him
correct_answer: Jesus refuses political seizure and offers himself as the true gift from heaven
explanation: He withdraws from forced kingship and later presents himself as the bread from heaven (John 6:15, 32-35).

prompt: In the flow of John 6, what does eating Jesus' flesh and drinking his blood signify?
choices_json: Receiving Jesus fully in his self-giving life and death
Copying the miracle meal each time the feast returns
Trusting the boy who first supplied the loaves
Waiting for Moses to send bread again from heaven
correct_answer: Receiving Jesus fully in his self-giving life and death
explanation: The discourse presses hearers to depend on Jesus himself for life, not merely on bread or signs (John 6:53-56).

prompt: How does the saying about the Son of Man ascending where he was before deepen John 6?
choices_json: The one who came down from heaven will also return there
The crowd will soon watch Jesus ascend from the mountain
The disciples must ascend spiritually before they can believe
The ascent refers to the baskets being lifted into the boat
correct_answer: The one who came down from heaven will also return there
explanation: Jesus links his heavenly descent with a future ascent, reinforcing his origin from above (John 6:38, 62).

prompt: What does "The Spirit gives life; the flesh profits nothing" clarify in John 6?
choices_json: Jesus' words must be received as life-giving Spirit, not as crude literalism
Physical bread is sinful, so no believer should eat ordinary meals
The miracle of the loaves failed because the crowd still hungered
Only angels can understand the teaching given in Capernaum
correct_answer: Jesus' words must be received as life-giving Spirit, not as crude literalism
explanation: Jesus immediately explains that his words are spirit and life, clarifying the scandal of the discourse (John 6:63).

prompt: Why is Peter's confession placed immediately after many disciples leave in John 6?
choices_json: John contrasts departing offense with persevering faith in Jesus' words
Peter is being appointed to replace Judas in the twelve at once
The chapter is shifting from Galilee back to the pool at Bethesda
Peter is correcting Philip's answer about buying bread
correct_answer: John contrasts departing offense with persevering faith in Jesus' words
explanation: After many walk away, Peter voices the faith of those who remain with Jesus (John 6:66-69).

prompt: How does the mention of Judas at the end of John 6 sharpen the chapter's message?
choices_json: Even within the chosen twelve, unbelief can hide near Jesus
Judas is praised for staying when the larger crowd departs
The betrayal note explains why the baskets were twelve in number
It shows that every disciple except Judas understood the discourse
correct_answer: Even within the chosen twelve, unbelief can hide near Jesus
explanation: John closes the chapter by noting that one of the twelve would betray Jesus (John 6:70-71).

prompt: How does John 6 advance the Gospel's theme of the sent Son?
choices_json: Jesus repeatedly presents himself as the one sent down by the living Father
The chapter replaces the language of sending with only kingdom language
The sent theme appears only in Peter's confession, not in Jesus' words
Sending refers mainly to the crowd traveling from Tiberias
correct_answer: Jesus repeatedly presents himself as the one sent down by the living Father
explanation: The discourse keeps returning to Jesus as the one the Father sent and gave (John 6:29, 38-40, 57).

prompt: Which feast was at hand in John 7?
choices_json: The Feast of Booths
The Passover
The Feast of Dedication
Pentecost
correct_answer: The Feast of Booths
explanation: John says the Feast of Booths was at hand (John 7:2).

prompt: Where was Jesus walking before going up to the feast in John 7?
choices_json: Galilee
Judea
Samaria
Bethany
correct_answer: Galilee
explanation: At the start of the chapter, Jesus was walking in Galilee (John 7:1).

prompt: Who urged Jesus to leave and go into Judea in John 7?
choices_json: His brothers
The twelve
The Pharisees
The officers
correct_answer: His brothers
explanation: Jesus' brothers urged him to go into Judea so his disciples could see his works (John 7:3).

prompt: How did Jesus go up to the feast after his brothers had gone in John 7?
choices_json: Not publicly, but in secret
With a large crowd from Galilee
In the company of the officers
At the beginning of the feast in daylight
correct_answer: Not publicly, but in secret
explanation: After his brothers went up, Jesus also went up, not publicly but in secret (John 7:10).

prompt: Where did Jesus begin teaching in the middle of the feast in John 7?
choices_json: In the temple
In the synagogue at Capernaum
At the pool of Bethesda
On the Mount of Olives
correct_answer: In the temple
explanation: In the middle of the feast, Jesus went up into the temple and taught (John 7:14).

prompt: What invitation did Jesus cry out on the last day of the feast in John 7?
choices_json: If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink
If anyone is sick, let him enter the pool
If anyone listens, let him go to Galilee
If anyone hungers, let him gather the bread that remains
correct_answer: If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink
explanation: Jesus cried out, "If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink" (John 7:37).

prompt: Which Pharisee spoke up about hearing a man before judging him in John 7?
choices_json: Nicodemus
Caiaphas
Gamaliel, a respected teacher among the council
Andrew
correct_answer: Nicodemus
explanation: Nicodemus asked whether their law judged a man before hearing him (John 7:50-51).

prompt: Why would Jesus not walk in Judea at the start of John 7?
choices_json: Because the Jews sought to kill him
Because the roads were blocked for the feast
Because his disciples had already left him
Because the temple officers were guarding Galilee
correct_answer: Because the Jews sought to kill him
explanation: John says Jesus would not walk in Judea because the Jews sought to kill him (John 7:1).

prompt: What reason did Jesus give for the world's hatred toward him in John 7?
choices_json: He testified that its works were evil
He refused to attend the feast at all
He healed too many people in Jerusalem
He told the crowds to stop searching the law
correct_answer: He testified that its works were evil
explanation: Jesus said the world hated him because he testified that its works were evil (John 7:7).

prompt: What did Jesus say about his time compared with his brothers' time in John 7?
choices_json: My time has not yet come, but your time is always ready
My time is fulfilled, but yours will come later
Our time is hidden until the last day of the feast arrives
My time and your time are both under Moses
correct_answer: My time has not yet come, but your time is always ready
explanation: Jesus contrasts his not-yet time with their always-ready time (John 7:6).

prompt: What standard did Jesus command in John 7:24?
choices_json: Judge with righteous judgment
Judge only according to Moses
Judge by the number of signs seen
Judge no one until the feast is over
correct_answer: Judge with righteous judgment
explanation: Jesus said, "Don't judge according to appearance, but judge righteous judgment" (John 7:24).

prompt: What did the officers say when they returned without arresting Jesus in John 7?
choices_json: No man ever spoke like this man!
He slipped away before we reached him.
The crowd hid him among the worshipers.
Nicodemus told us to wait for daylight.
correct_answer: No man ever spoke like this man!
explanation: The officers answered, "No man ever spoke like this man!" (John 7:46).

prompt: What hometown objection did some in the crowd raise against Jesus being the Christ in John 7?
choices_json: They said the Christ could not come out of Galilee
They said the Christ could not come from David's line
They said the Christ must first heal at Bethesda
They said the Christ would never appear at a feast
correct_answer: They said the Christ could not come out of Galilee
explanation: Some objected that the Christ would not come out of Galilee (John 7:41).

prompt: According to the crowd in John 7:42, from what village should the Christ come?
choices_json: Bethlehem
Nazareth
Bethany
Capernaum
correct_answer: Bethlehem
explanation: The crowd says the Christ should come from Bethlehem, the village of David (John 7:42).

prompt: What does Jesus' quiet arrival and later public teaching show about his actions in John 7?
choices_json: He moves by the Father's timing, not by human pressure
He changes his plans because his brothers persuade him
He fears the crowds more than he fears the rulers
He no longer wants anyone to know his works
correct_answer: He moves by the Father's timing, not by human pressure
explanation: Jesus refuses his brothers' timetable, then appears publicly when the feast is underway (John 7:6-10, 14).

prompt: How does Jesus use the Sabbath circumcision example in his argument in John 7?
choices_json: He shows that lawful Sabbath action should make whole-man healing understandable
He argues that circumcision should be ended during feast days
He says Moses' law matters less than the temple rituals and mercy
He admits the healing at Bethesda had broken the law
correct_answer: He shows that lawful Sabbath action should make whole-man healing understandable
explanation: Jesus argues that if circumcision can happen on the Sabbath, making a man whole on the Sabbath should not anger them (John 7:22-23).

prompt: What does the chapter's repeated debate about where Jesus is from reveal in John 7?
choices_json: People know surface facts about Jesus but miss his true sender
The crowds have accurate knowledge and therefore believe quickly
John wants to correct a mistake about the route from Galilee
The officers alone understand Jesus' family history
correct_answer: People know surface facts about Jesus but miss his true sender
explanation: The crowd talks about Galilee and Bethlehem, while Jesus presses the deeper issue of the one who sent him (John 7:27-29, 41-42).

prompt: Why does Jesus say his hearers will seek him and not find him in John 7?
choices_json: His departure to the one who sent him lies beyond their present grasp
He plans to hide again somewhere in Galilee after the feast
The officers will remove him from the temple before night falls
The crowd will forget him once the feast water ceremony is finished
correct_answer: His departure to the one who sent him lies beyond their present grasp
explanation: Jesus says he is going to the one who sent him, and they cannot come where he is going (John 7:33-34).

prompt: What does Jesus' promise of living water mean in John 7's own explanation?
choices_json: Believers will receive the Spirit after Jesus is glorified
Pilgrims will find fresh water in Jerusalem all year
The last day of the feast removes the need for temple offerings
The crowds will remember the pool of Bethesda and return there
correct_answer: Believers will receive the Spirit after Jesus is glorified
explanation: John explains that Jesus was speaking about the Spirit, who would be given after Jesus was glorified (John 7:37-39).

prompt: What does Nicodemus' response to the Pharisees reveal in John 7?
choices_json: At least one ruler insists on hearing Jesus fairly before judging him
Nicodemus fully confesses Jesus as Messiah before the council that night
Nicodemus agrees that no prophet can come from Galilee
Nicodemus leads the officers back to arrest Jesus himself
correct_answer: At least one ruler insists on hearing Jesus fairly before judging him
explanation: Nicodemus asks whether their law judges a man before first hearing from him (John 7:50-51).

prompt: Why does John record such divided reactions to Jesus throughout John 7?
choices_json: The same words force the crowd to take sides about his identity
The people agree on Jesus but fear the officers' report
The divisions are only about which feast should be kept
The chapter is mainly describing confusion over travel plans
correct_answer: The same words force the crowd to take sides about his identity
explanation: John repeatedly shows debate, murmuring, belief, and rejection around Jesus' teaching in the feast setting (John 7:12, 25-31, 40-43).

prompt: How does the Feast of Booths setting sharpen Jesus' cry about thirst and living water in John 7?
choices_json: The feast context lets Jesus present himself as the true source of what the celebration longs for
The feast proves that only Jerusalem can provide the life Jesus describes
The feast is mentioned only to explain why his brothers traveled first
The feast setting has no direct link to Jesus' words about water
correct_answer: The feast context lets Jesus present himself as the true source of what the celebration longs for
explanation: John places the living water invitation on the great day of the feast, making Jesus the focus of the chapter's hope (John 7:2, 37-39).

prompt: Why does John note that even Jesus' brothers did not believe in him in John 7?
choices_json: Rejection of Jesus is shown as beginning close to home, not only in the rulers
The brothers are being prepared to replace the Pharisees as leaders
Their unbelief explains why the officers later return empty-handed
John wants to identify which brother first reported him to the council
correct_answer: Rejection of Jesus is shown as beginning close to home, not only in the rulers
explanation: John explicitly notes his brothers' unbelief before showing wider public division and official hostility (John 7:5, 12-13, 32).

prompt: What does Jesus' statement, "My teaching is not mine," reveal in John 7?
choices_json: His authority comes from the Father who sent him
His teaching changes according to the crowd at each feast
His words are only a repetition of what the brothers said
His message matters less than the signs people expect
correct_answer: His authority comes from the Father who sent him
explanation: Jesus grounds his teaching in the one who sent him, not in self-promotion (John 7:16-18).

prompt: How does the repeated line that no one laid hands on Jesus function in John 7?
choices_json: It shows human hostility cannot outrun the hour set for Jesus
It means the rulers had secretly decided to follow him instead
It proves the officers could not find Jesus in the temple courts
It shows that Nicodemus had legal control over the arrest
correct_answer: It shows human hostility cannot outrun the hour set for Jesus
explanation: John explains the failed arrest by saying Jesus' hour had not yet come (John 7:30, 44).

prompt: What literary role do the officers' words, "No man ever spoke like this man," play in John 7?
choices_json: Unexpected witnesses underline the power of Jesus' speech amid official rejection
The officers are quoting Psalm language used earlier in the feast
Their report settles the debate and ends the division in Jerusalem
The line explains that the officers had become disciples in private
correct_answer: Unexpected witnesses underline the power of Jesus' speech amid official rejection
explanation: The very officers sent to arrest Jesus return impressed by his words, deepening the chapter's divided responses (John 7:32, 45-46).

prompt: How does John 7 connect willingness to do God's will with recognizing Jesus' teaching?
choices_json: Obedience is presented as the path to discerning whether his teaching is from God
Only trained teachers of the law can judge Jesus' doctrine correctly
Miracles matter more than moral willingness in testing a teacher
Recognition comes only after a ruler publicly approves him before the crowd
correct_answer: Obedience is presented as the path to discerning whether his teaching is from God
explanation: Jesus says that the one who desires to do God's will will know whether the teaching is from God (John 7:17).

prompt: How does the argument about Galilee and Bethlehem function in John 7?
choices_json: Partial knowledge and confident assumptions keep many from right judgment
The crowd solves the question and therefore ends the division quickly
John is correcting the census records of the Feast of Booths
The point is that Bethlehem had become spiritually unimportant
correct_answer: Partial knowledge and confident assumptions keep many from right judgment
explanation: The crowd argues from incomplete information about Jesus' origin and ends up divided over him (John 7:41-43, 52).

prompt: How does John 7 connect thirst, coming to Jesus, and believing in him?
choices_json: Faith is pictured as coming to Jesus to receive the life he alone gives
Belief is treated as one more purification rite during the feast
Thirst refers only to physical need after traveling to Jerusalem
Coming to Jesus is postponed until after his brothers believe
correct_answer: Faith is pictured as coming to Jesus to receive the life he alone gives
explanation: Jesus calls the thirsty to come and drink, and John ties that promise to believing in him (John 7:37-38).

prompt: What does John's own comment in John 7:39 add to the meaning of the living water saying?
choices_json: The promised water refers to the Spirit given after Jesus is glorified
The saying predicts that Jerusalem's pools will overflow during the feast
The crowd should remember Moses striking the rock in the desert
The words refer only to the tears of those who oppose Jesus
correct_answer: The promised water refers to the Spirit given after Jesus is glorified
explanation: John explains that Jesus was speaking about the Spirit, who would be given after his glorification (John 7:39).

prompt: How does John 7 deepen John's theme of Jesus' heavenly origin?
choices_json: People think they know where Jesus is from, but they do not know the one who sent him
John drops the origin theme here and focuses only on legal disputes
The brothers explain Jesus' origin more clearly than Jesus does himself
The rulers accept his origin but reject his teaching for other reasons
correct_answer: People think they know where Jesus is from, but they do not know the one who sent him
explanation: The chapter contrasts familiar assumptions about Jesus' earthly background with his claim to be sent from the true one (John 7:27-29).

prompt: Why is Nicodemus' brief protest significant in the wider Gospel?
choices_json: He moves from private night questions toward a public, if cautious, defense
He fully leaves the Pharisees and joins the twelve in this scene
He proves that every ruler already believes in Jesus secretly
He is appointed as the official judge over Jesus' case
correct_answer: He moves from private night questions toward a public, if cautious, defense
explanation: Nicodemus, who first came by night, now speaks up publicly for due hearing (John 7:50-51).

prompt: What does John 7 suggest about the effect of social pressure on belief and speech?
choices_json: Fear of the Jews keeps many from speaking openly or judging honestly
Public pressure has little effect once the feast begins in Jerusalem
The crowd becomes bolder because the rulers openly welcome debate
Only Jesus' brothers are silenced by fear in this chapter
correct_answer: Fear of the Jews keeps many from speaking openly or judging honestly
explanation: John says people feared speaking openly, and the pressure of the rulers shapes the chapter's debates (John 7:13, 45-49).

prompt: How does Jesus both fulfill and unsettle the Feast of Booths in John 7?
choices_json: He uses the feast's setting to point its hopes back to himself
He refuses every feast symbol and speaks only about future judgment
He keeps the feast quietly but never teaches during it
He turns the feast into a debate about temple architecture
correct_answer: He uses the feast's setting to point its hopes back to himself
explanation: Jesus teaches in the feast and turns its thirst and expectation toward himself (John 7:14, 37-38).

prompt: Why does John 7 end without resolving the division around Jesus?
choices_json: The unresolved split shows that revelation intensifies decision rather than removing it
The chapter was originally missing the ending of the arrest report
John wants to save every answer until Nicodemus speaks again in a later public scene
The division ends because everyone finally agrees he is the Christ
correct_answer: The unresolved split shows that revelation intensifies decision rather than removing it
explanation: The chapter closes with division still in place, showing how Jesus' words force judgment rather than instant consensus (John 7:40-43, 45-52).
